narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your early and later school grades, LSAT score, family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, and it calculated a 51.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass confidently. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the average of around the 5th or 6th decile—which signaled to the model a concerning dip in later academic performance. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which had a strong favorable influence by exceeding the norm and suggesting underlying capability. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also nudged the prediction toward failure, though less powerfully, as it was similarly low compared to peers' averages near the middle of the pack. While your middle family income bracket and full-time study plans aligned with most applicants, and your LSAT of 29 was a weaker area, it wasn't among the top drivers here. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile shows early promise overshadowed by weaker school rankings, making the model cautious despite your stronger college record. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an individual, but a data-driven risk assessment based on patterns from thousands of similar cases. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 37 average of successful students, or strengthening recent academic showings through additional coursework or a post-bac program. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your full-time commitment and GPA more heavily. With focused improvements, especially in those school deciles, you can reshape your profile for stronger predictions next cycle. Keep pushing—many overcome these hurdles and succeed.
